perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall pr omptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form repr o- duced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information di s- closed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contempla ted in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may r equest it to the Recipient .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall return or destroy all copies (in whatever form repr o- duced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information di s- closed under this Agreement, only at the sole discretion of the Recipient, and no sooner than 18 months following (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contempla ted in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may r equest it to the Recipient .
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return/destroy clause to allow the Recipient to delay returning confidential information for at least 18 months introduces a legal risk, as some trade secret laws provide remedies based on the duration of misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act of 1996, 18 U.S. Code § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows the Recipient to retain confidential information for an extended period, which could be construed as misappropriation under the Economic Espionage Act, particularly if used to benefit a foreign entity or cause economic harm. The law protects trade secrets and provides remedies for their misappropriation, and delaying the return/destruction increases the potential for misuse.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a delay of at least 18 months before the Recipient is obligated to return or destroy confidential information, and grants them sole discretion over when they do so. This directly contradicts the original text which required prompt action upon specific triggers. The Law Explanation suggests this delay could be construed as misappropriation under the Economic Espionage Act, particularly if it causes economic harm. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 and cites 18 U.S.C. 1839, suggesting that delaying the return of confidential information could potentially violate trade secret protections under this act. The change significantly weakens the Discloser's ability to protect their confidential information, and could lead to potential legal ramifications, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ] term. Notwithstanding th e foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ] term. Notwithstanding th e foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l expire 1 year after termination of the agreement, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligation to 1 year can conflict with trade secret laws that protect trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide an economic advantage. Some trade secrets retain value for much longer than a year.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the confidentiality period to one year, the modified clause conflicts with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which provides for injunctive relief and damages for the misappropriation of trade secrets as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret. A one-year limit is insufficient to protect trade secrets that retain their value and secrecy beyond that period, creating a legal conflict if misappropriation occurs after the one-year term.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text limits the confidentiality period to one year, which directly contradicts the principles of trade secret law. The Law Explanation states that this one-year limit is insufficient to protect trade secrets that retain their value and secrecy beyond that period, creating a legal conflict. The scraped law snippet defines the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) as providing a legal framework for improved trade secret protection to industry. By limiting the confidentiality period, the modified text undermines the UTSA's aim to protect trade secrets for as long as they remain valuable and secret, as misappropriation could occur after the one-year limit, leading to a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have ju risdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. Any disputes arising out of or in connection with this agreement shall be resolved exclusively through binding arbitration in a secret location, with no right to appeal the arbitrator's decision.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing binding arbitration with no appeal in a secret location may conflict with laws that guarantee due process and the right to a fair legal remedy, particularly in disputes involving significant sums or intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) favors arbitration, Section 10 allows for vacating an arbitration award under specific circumstances, such as corruption, fraud, or misconduct by the arbitrator. By eliminating the right to appeal, the modified clause attempts to circumvent these FAA provisions, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable if it denies a party a fundamentally fair hearing or due process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces binding arbitration with no right to appeal, which contradicts the FAA provisions allowing for vacating an arbitration award under specific circumstances. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 9 U.S. Code § 10, outlines grounds for vacating an award, including corruption, fraud, or misconduct by the arbitrators. By eliminating the right to appeal, the modified clause attempts to circumvent these FAA provisions, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable if it denies a party a fundamentally fair hearing or due process, which is a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 In the context of discussions, preparations or negotiations, the Discloser may disclose Confidential Information to the Recipient . The Recipient agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connectio n with purposes contemplat ed in this Agreement and not to use it for any other purpose or without the prior written consent of the Discloser .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 In the context of discussions, preparations or negotiations, the Discloser may disclose Confidential Information to the Recipient . The Recipient agrees to use the Confidential Information solely in connectio n with purposes contemplat ed in this Agreement and may use it for any other purpose without the prior written consent of the Discloser, unless explicitly prohibited by federal statute.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Recipient to use Confidential Information for any purpose unless explicitly prohibited by federal statute, undermining the very purpose of the NDA. This could conflict with state trade secret laws, which provide broader protection than just federal statutes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly conflicts with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). CUTSA defines 'misappropriation' as the use of a trade secret without consent. By allowing the Recipient to use confidential information for any purpose not explicitly prohibited by federal statute, the clause effectively permits misappropriation of trade secrets protected under CUTSA, directly contradicting the law's core purpose of protecting trade secrets from unauthorized use.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Recipient to use Confidential Information for any purpose not explicitly prohibited by federal statute. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) because it effectively permits misappropriation of trade secrets protected under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code (CIV) 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. This act protects trade secrets from unauthorized use, and the changed text directly contradicts this protection by allowing use unless prohibited by federal statute, not state law which has broader protections.
